retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: ACOG and WHO recommend considering a single repeat course of antenatal corticosteroids (ie, "rescue steroids") in individuals who have received 1 previous course of steroids at least 7 to 14 days prior and are currently. A rescue course may consist of either a standard 48-hour regimen described above or a single dose of betamethasone, which evidence suggests is also effective after initial standard therapy. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: When given, 1 of 2 antenatal corticosteroid regiments should be used as follows: Two doses of betamethasone 12 mg intramuscularly (IM), given 24 hours apart Four doses of dexamethasone 6 mg IM, given every 12 hours [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment during pregnancy: Successful conception is typically achievable with adequate disease control. As dexamethasone crosses the placenta, hydrocortisone or prednisolone should be used during pregnancy. As with other cases of primary adrenal insufficiency, a 20% to 40% increase in steroid dose is necessary during the second and third trimesters. Stress dosing should be administered during labor and delivery. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Corticosteroid Adverse Effects -- Indications -- Prophylactic
retrieved_docs.content: Organ transplant (to prevent rejection due to their immunosuppressive action) Preterm delivery (to allow fetal lung maturity)

retrieved_docs.title: 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Long-Term Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment during pregnancy : Adequate disease control typically leads to successful conception. As dexamethasone crosses the placenta, hydrocortisone or prednisolone should be used during pregnancy. Concordant to any other case of primary adrenal insufficiency, an increase of 20% to 40% in steroid dose is required during the second and third trimesters. Stress dosing should be administered during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Triamcinolone -- Administration -- Special Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnant considerations: Clinicians must weigh the risks and benefits, especially in the first trimester or with long-term use. Conflicting human data shows a risk of orofacial cleft, possible low birth weight, and premature birth based on limited data from other corticosteroids.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG) Green-Top Guideline Number 74 recommends offering antenatal corticosteroids up until 34 6/7 weeks EGA. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The beneficial effect of antenatal corticosteroid use after 34 weeks of gestation is controversial due to the lack of information in long term developmental outcomes. [44] Maternal antenatal corticosteroids are recommended for possible preterm delivery in the next seven days between 23rd and 34th week gestational age. [45] Some institutions offer antenatal corticosteroids at 22 weeks if anticipating delivery within the next week. Despite multiple interventions targeting various etiologies, the goal of preventing preterm birth remains elusive.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: Antenatal corticosteroids improve neonatal outcomes when given to individuals at high risk of PTB; these are most effective between 48 hours and 7 days before PTB. [2] For these reasons, antenatal corticosteroids are universally recommended for patients at risk of PTB between viability and 34 weeks EGA. ACOG, the Society for Maternal-Fetal Medicine (SMFM), and the WAPM-PMF guidelines recommend considering antenatal corticosteroids in the periviable period, down to 22 0/7 weeks if neonatal resuscitation is planned. [2] However, beyond 34 weeks, recommendations vary across professional organizations.

retrieved_docs.title: Prevention, Evaluation, and Management of Coronavirus (COVID-19) in Pregnancy and Puerperium -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Concomitant diseases and Vaccination Decisions
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have been advised to take inhaled corticosteroids or systemic steroids for other systemic pathologies must continue their steroids irrespective of the COVID-19 infection. [54] [55] [56] However, patients who have been on long-term systemic steroids may be unable to mount an adequate immune response once vaccinated. [57] Hence, these patients should receive an additional dose of vaccine for the mRNA schedule. Additionally, a booster dose is indicated as per routine for all patients who have completed a vaccination course 6 months after the last dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guideline [NG25] recommends considering antenatal corticosteroids between 34 and 35 6/7 weeks EGA.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical Corticosteroids -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: A systemic review suggests that maternal use of topical corticosteroids, regardless of potency, is not associated with significant adverse pregnancy outcomes. However, there could be a potential link between cumulative doses of potent corticosteroids and low birth weight; additional research is required. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Eclampsia
retrieved_docs.content: Seizure management Magnesium sulfate : Magnesium sulfate is preferred over other antiseizure treatments for eclamptic seizure prevention. While most seizures are self-limiting, magnesium sulfate is administered primarily to prevent further seizure activity, not to halt the current seizure. [2] The recommended standard loading dose is 6 g intravenous (IV) over 15 to 20 min, followed by a 2 g/hour maintenance dose. In patients without IV access, a loading dose of magnesium sulfate 10 g intramuscularly, followed by a maintenance dose of 5 g intramuscularly every 4 hours, may be used as an alternative regimen. [11] [2] In patients with contraindications to magnesium sulfate (eg, myasthenia gravis, hypocalcemia, renal failure, cardiac ischemia, heart block, or myocarditis), alternative antiseizure therapies (eg, phenytoin and diazepam) may be considered. [2] Levetiracetam may be preferable in patients with myasthenia gravis, a condition that may be exacerbated by phenytoin. [18] Recurrent seizures : If convulsions recur, clinicians may consider administering an additional 2 to 4 g of magnesium sulfate over 5 min. [2] If seizures persist after 20 minues, or more than 2 seizures have occurred despite magnesium therapy, lorazepam 4 mg IV over 3 to 5 minutes is recommended. [11] Other alternative seizure interventions that may be utilized include: Sodium amobarbital : 250 mg IV in 3 minutes [2] Diazepam : Loading dose of 10 mg IV over 2 min, then maintenance dose of 40 mg in 500 mL normal saline for 24 hours, followed by 20 mg in 500 mL for the next 24 hours [19] Phenytoin : Loading dose of 10 mg/kg initially in 30 minutes, then 2 hours later administer 5 mg/kg over 10 minutes, followed by a maintenance dose of 300 mg IV over 10 minutes for 24 hours from the last seizure [19] Fetal monitoring : During seizures, fetal heart rate decelerations, bradycardia, or tachycardia may occur secondary to maternal hypoxia and hypercapnia. Uterine contractions can increase but usually resolve once the maternal condition stabilizes. [2] [11] Postpartum management : Magnesium sulfate is recommended to prevent eclamptic seizures in postpartum patients with new-onset hypertension and neurologic symptoms (eg, headaches and blurred vision) or preeclampsia with severe features. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The Society of Obstetricians and Gynecologists of Canada (SOGC) Technical Update No. 439 states that antenatal corticosteroids should be considered in patients between 34 and 36 6/7 weeks EGA. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid that may be employed in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). However, its use is contraindicated in the setting of pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Clinicians should therefore balance the potential benefit of triamcinolone for managing multiple sclerosis against the risk of adverse effects in pregnant patients and consider alternative therapies when a pregnancy is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 5 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: Ketorolac, a potent non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory medication, is contraindicated in the setting of الولادة (delivery), because its use during labor can increase the risk of bleeding and other obstetric complications. The event of الولادة occurs after نواتج متبقية من الحمل, indicating that the act of giving birth follows the residual products or outcomes of the preceding pregnancy. Recognizing that ketorolac should not be administered during الولادة and understanding the temporal sequence from نواتج متبقية من الحمل to delivery are essential for safe and effective obstetric patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1138
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolon is classified as a member of the “Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations” group, which in turn is a subclass of the broader “corticosteroids, other combinations” category; this hierarchy also includes “Corticosteroids, potent, other combinations” and “Corticosteroids, weak, other combinations” as sibling subclasses. As a moderately potent corticosteroid, triamcinolon may be used to treat multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي), but it is contraindicated in the context of pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Hydrocortisone sodium succinate, another corticosteroid formulation, is considered appropriate for use during pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة) and is also indicated for the management of circulatory collapse (الانهيار في الدورة الدموية). Understanding these relationships—drug class membership, potency levels, therapeutic indications, and contraindications—helps clinicians select the right corticosteroid for specific conditions while avoiding use in situations such as pregnancy where triamcinolon is unsafe.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2086
retrieved_docs.content: Marginal placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage is a condition that can occur during الولادة (delivery). The medication ketorolak is contraindicated with disease during الولادة, indicating it should not be used in this setting. Various labor complications are associated with الولادة: تعسر المخاض (difficult labor) and عسر ولادة متعلق بعنق الرحم (cervical dystocia) may arise during the process. Additionally, الولادة can be characterized by مخاض طويل (prolonged labor) or مخاض محفز (stimulated labor), reflecting different patterns of uterine activity. After الولادة, نواتج متبقية من الحمل (residual pregnancy outcomes) may persist, requiring postpartum monitoring. Understanding these interrelated conditions and their timing relative to delivery is essential for optimal obstetric management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy (الحمل) is a gynecologic condition and is closely linked to the concept of “Zwangerschap als bijkomstige bevinding,” which denotes pregnancy as an incidental finding and is essentially synonymous with the term. Certain medications are contraindicated during pregnancy; specifically, norethindron, testosterone, tetracycline phosphate complex, and triptorelin should not be administered to pregnant individuals because of potential fetal risk. A septuplet pregnancy represents an extreme form of pregnancy in which the mother carries seven fetuses, illustrating the variability in pregnancy presentations. Understanding the gynecologic nature of pregnancy, its possible identification as an incidental finding, and the drug contraindications is essential for safe patient care and appropriate management of both typical and high‑order multiple pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrexate is classified as a non‑steroidal abortifacient, indicating that it belongs to the group of medications used for medical termination of pregnancy that are not based on steroid hormones. This categorization highlights methotrexate’s role in reproductive health as a pharmacologic agent distinct from steroidal abortifacients, and underscores its importance in therapeutic protocols that require non‑steroidal mechanisms for inducing abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2889
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with الولادة (birth), نواتج متبقية من الحمل (post‑pregnancy residual outcomes), and gestation twin (twin gestation). In this context, الولادة is described as occurring after نواتج متبقية من الحمل, indicating that the birth event follows the residual outcomes of pregnancy. Additionally, the medication ketorolak is contraindicated in the disease state of الولادة, meaning that ketorolak should not be used in patients experiencing or at risk for birth‑related conditions. Together, these relationships highlight the relevance of database information to various aspects of pregnancy and delivery, the temporal sequence between postpartum residuals and birth, and the need to avoid ketorolak when الولادة is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 718
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a glucocorticoid that can be employed in the management of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). It belongs to a broader class that includes both inhaled glucocorticoids for obstructive airway disease and systemic glucocorticoids. Inhaled glucocorticoids for obstructive airway disease comprise agents such as prednison, hydrocortisone sodium succinate, prednisolone sodium succinate, and triamcinolone diacetate. Systemic glucocorticoids encompass drugs such as prednison, meprednisone, and paramethason. Triamcinolone and its related formulations are contraindicated in pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is also contraindicated in pregnancy and is indicated for the treatment of circulatory collapse (الانهيار في الدورة الدموية). The relationships among inhaled and systemic glucocorticoids reflect overlapping therapeutic categories, with certain agents appearing in both groups, underscoring the importance of careful selection based on the route of administration and patient‑specific factors such as pregnancy status and hemodynamic stability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 874
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy (الحمل) is the condition in which a baby is coming out, representing the process of labor and delivery. Certain medications are contraindicated during pregnancy: elagolix, ethylestrenol, and norethindrone enanthate are specifically listed as contraindicated with pregnancy, and the tetracycline phosphate complex and triptorelin are identified as drugs that should not be used in pregnant patients. A septuplet pregnancy is a particular type of pregnancy that involves multiple fetuses (seven children), highlighting the need for special clinical attention. Recognizing these drug contraindications and the unique considerations of high‑order multiple gestations is essential for ensuring safe maternal and fetal outcomes.
